sections.title: A: Multiple Choice (Wikipedia text)
sections.instructions: Choose the correct answer (A, B, C or D) for each gap.

items.sentence: Before Wikipedia, it was (1) ...... as read that encyclopaedias were written by paid experts.
In other (2) ...... , before Wikipedia, the 'reader' of an encyclopaedia had no control over the content.
The idea has certainly (3) ...... on and, for millions of people, Wikipedia (4) ...... a vital need.
The idea has certainly (3) ...... on and, for millions of people, Wikipedia (4) ...... a vital need.
Wikipedia's founders (5) ...... that, on the whole, the information on the site is no less accurate than more traditional encyclopaedias.
Despite this, critics have (6) ...... the site for its inaccuracies.
Articles on the site have certainly (7) ...... things which are not in fact true.
However, although these have received wide media (8) ...... , they essentially (9) ...... the problem.
However, although these have received wide media (8) ...... , they essentially (9) ...... the problem.
Most people have little (10) ...... how many inaccuracies there are in traditional printed encyclopaedias too.

sections.title: B: Key Word Transformation
sections.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence:
My dad’s not happy with me at all because he heard me swearing. **books**
I ................................................................. because he heard me swearing.
items.answer: am in my dad's bad books

items.sentence:
There’s no need for me to tell you that you’ll be paid handsomely for this. **saying**
It ................................................................. that you’ll be paid handsomely for this.

He hasn’t announced anything, but my take on the situation is that the Prime Minister’s going to resign soon. **lines**
He hasn’t announced anything, but ................................................................. , I’d say that the Prime Minister’s going to resign soon.

Tom and Barbara aren’t talking to each other at the moment. **terms**
Tom and Barbara aren’t ................................................................. at the moment.

Persuading Adrian to lie for you was a bad thing to do. **talked**
You should ................................................................. for you.
items.answer: not have talked Adrian into lying

items.sentence:
I’m sure she discussed this with Bob first. **word**
She ................................................................. this first.
items.answer: must have had a word with Bob about

items.sentence:
I’m sure Jasmin didn’t misunderstand, Carol. **stick**
Jasmin can’t ................................................................., Carol.
items.answer: have got the wrong end of the stick

items.sentence:
It’s not necessary for you to promise – I know I can trust you. **word**
You ................................................................. – I know I can trust you.

sections.title: C: Word Formation
sections.instructions: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals.

items.sentence: Under her fine ...................... , circulation has increased by 100,000. **EDIT**
It’s pure ........................ - there’s no evidence to support these allegations at all. **HEAR**
The factory’s closure will have far-reaching ....................... for the whole region. **IMPLY**
Saying Shakespeare was ‘quite a good writer’ is a bit of a/an ....................., don’t you think? **STATE**
Greg’s not a very ...................... person, really. **TALK**
There’s a/an ...................... rule in this office that we all go out for a drink together after work on a Friday. **WRITE**
In formal writing, it is unusual to make use of the ...................... mark to express surprise. **EXCLAIM**
You are ...................... forbidden to use a calculator in this examination. **EXPRESS**

sections.title: D: One-word gap fill (phrasal verbs / expressions)
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: What day of the week does that magazine come ...... out on?
Mum took some persuading, but we managed to talk her ...... round in the end.
I can’t give you permission now – I’ll have to talk it ...... over with your mother first.
I know it’s a secret but you can tell me. I won’t let ...... slip, I promise!
I stupidly blurted ...... out in front of Rachel that it’s your fortieth birthday next week.
I’ve learnt all my lines for the play now, but there’s always the fear that you’ll dry ...... up when you actually get on stage.
It’s time somebody spoke ...... out loudly and clearly against this war.
The Minister was shouted ...... down by hecklers and was unable to finish her speech.
You ………………… that film last week – it was only released yesterday.
………………… we better get an expert’s opinion on this before we make a decision?
I’d love ………………… afford to take a month or two off.
They’re definitely not going to call now, so we might ………………… go to bed.
………………… stand up when the teacher entered the room at your school?
Tamsin ………………… to her graduation ceremony, but she decided not to in the end.
You ………………… have told us you were going to be a bit late!
Thankfully we ………………… another ticket as Jean didn’t turn up.
